prompt: Which work specifies an exact traveler's allowance for an ascetic short of provisions on a journey, permitting him to take 20 cucumbers, 1 wax gourd, one handful of rice, 1 jackfruit, 5 kalvi, and likewise sentul fruit from an abandoned garden — without being called a thief? Quote the passage verbatim and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The combination of the exact numbered list (20 cucumbers, 1 wax gourd, 1 jackfruit, 5 kalvi) with Indonesian/Javanese fruits (kalvi, sentul) and the 'pari samuṣṭigraha' formula is unique to this Old Javanese ascetic-vow code. Sanskrit smṛtis treat traveler-theft generally but never with these specific Javanese fruit names or counts.

prompt: Which work names five 'primary' outcastes — the liquor-seller, the trapper (or insect-burner), the butcher (life-taker), the potter, and the goldsmith (metal-burner) — and declares that their stalls and houses must be avoided by an ascetic because they are 'worse than the eighteen caṇḍālas'? Quote the verse and the commentary's enumeration verbatim, and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The specific five-vs-eighteen caṇḍāla framing, where the goldsmith and potter are grouped with the butcher and liquor-seller as the primary outcastes 'worse than' a separate set of eighteen, is uniquely formulated in this Old Javanese vow-code. Sanskrit Manu/Yājñavalkya/Parāśara and the Javanese tattva texts in the corpus do not contain this exact five-versus-eighteen ascetic-avoidance rule with goldsmith and potter highlighted.

prompt: Which work warns an ascetic that, if he fails to protect his moral conduct, his own sacred thread will become a club to beat him in Yama's world, his sacred ash will become a mountain of fire burning his body, and his rosary will become an instrument of punishment with 'a hundred years of punishment for each bead'? Quote the passage verbatim and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The conceit of the ascetic's own four implements (sacred thread, sacred ash, rosary, water vessel) being literally transformed into instruments of torture in Yama's world — with the precise hundred-years-per-bead arithmetic for the rosary — is unique to this Old Javanese Pāśupata ascetic code. Sanskrit smṛtis describe hells and penances but not this specific implement-by-implement inversion.
